discussion_comments.comment_body: Check if we need to use dompurify here.

I think instead of using `url = url.replace(/\\/g, '\\\\').replace(/\(/g, '\\(').replace(/\)/g, '\\)');`, we might want to use [`CSS.escape()`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/CSS/escape_static).

It looks like we're trying to create valid [`CSS <url-token>`](https://www.w3.org/TR/css-syntax-3/#typedef-url-token), as these are passed unquoted to the [SVG/CSS `url()` function](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/url_function). `CSS.escape` will probably overly escape characters, but it's probably cleaner and describes what we want to do better.

There's other characters that are valid in a URI like `'`, but aren't valid in a CSS `<url-token>` so they should be escaped too.

@aloisklink I have updated the function to use CSS.escape and replaced hardcoded occurrences with common function getUrl. Please review.
cc: @sidharthv96
If this turns out to be an issue, we can also consider using a different url sanitization library, ill leave that up to the maintainers to decide :)

I am a bit worried that this might cause XSS attacks (although, it might be impossible without using URL unsafe characters like `"`, as they'll get converted to `%22`).

If somebody makes a malicious mermaid diagram with a malicious `javascript:xxxx` link, hosts it on example.com, and convinces somebody to click it, I believe the JavaScript will have access to the user's data on example.com

However, it's possible that https://github.com/cure53/DOMPurify will handle that for us, though, even if it isn't handled by the URI sanitizer library. I'll let @knsv chime in on it, since he probably knows more!

Bito Code Review Agent Run #e7aeaa - 08/12/2024, 02:50 pm
🔴 High importance
Issue: The 'ThemeVariables' interface does not enforce any constraints on the values of its properties. This could lead to invalid or inconsistent theme configurations.
Fix: Add validation logic to ensure that the values of the properties in the 'ThemeVariables' interface are valid. For example, ensure that color properties are valid CSS color strings and that font size and weight are within acceptable ranges.

@remcohaszing I didn't use [remark-breaks](https://github.com/remarkjs/remark-breaks) to add the `
` tags as using remark would need the code to be async, which might need larger changes in the codebase. Does this have any issue I might've missed?

I recommend against treating newlines as linebreaks in markdown, as they should be ignored according to commonmark.

If you really do want to, you can though. `remark-break` isn’t async. Unified processors have a `processSync` function alongside `process` that also works if all plugins are synchronous.

You could also copy the [logic](https://github.com/remarkjs/remark-breaks/blob/main/index.js#L18-L47) from `remark-breaks`. It’s not ideal, but it’s not that much either. See https://github.com/remarkjs/remark-breaks/issues/9
Sadly, The default example in the remark repo breaks. Should we get this in now to remove the react dependency in 10.2.0 release and release using unified later?

My guess is that the line above is `.use(remarkRehype)`

Try:

```ts
import * as hast from 'hast'
import * as mdast from 'mdast'
import { Plugin } from 'unified'

// …
.use(remarkRehype as Plugin<[], mdast.Root, hast.Root>)
```

It's not just a TS error. The output is an AST(?), not HTML. I tried first with a `@ts-ignore` This is the code. ```ts return unified() .use(remarkParse) .use(remarkBreaks) .use(remarkRehype) .use(rehypeSanitize) .use(rehypeStringify) .parseSync(markdown); ```

- [`parse`](https://github.com/unifiedjs/unified#processorparsefile) parses the input to AST.
- [`run`](https://github.com/unifiedjs/unified#processorruntree-file-done) / [`runSync`](https://github.com/unifiedjs/unified#processorrunsynctree-file) transforms the AST.
- [`stringify`](https://github.com/unifiedjs/unified#processorstringifytree-file) compiles the AST.
- `process` / `processSync` does `parse` → `run` → `compile`

> I recommend against treating newlines as linebreaks in markdown, as they should be ignored according to commonmark. I'd agree with @remcohaszing here. It's only some GitHub comments/description that have a weird markdown implementation where they treat newlines as `
`. Pretty much every normal markdown tool (and markdown spec) ignores newlines, and uses `\n\n` (two newlines) to make a `
`. I'd prefer if we remove this bit, but I don't know if it's too late to change without it being a breaking change though :sob: Maybe it's something to put into a Mermaid v11 wishlist if it is too late to change the behavior. --- Also, previously we were only outputting ``, ``, and `

` HTML tags. Now if we use `micromark`'s default HTML output, there will be a lot more HTML tags. It sounds like `micromark` is safe from XSS attacks, (see https://github.com/micromark/micromark/tree/main#security), but there might be a bunch of weird formatting issues. It might be better to stick the `micromark` library in a separate PR for more intensive review/testing.
